is an area which observes a uniform [standard time](/wiki/Standard_time "Standard time") for legal, commercial, and social purposes. Time zones tend to follow the boundaries between [countries](/wiki/Country "Country") and their [subdivisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") instead of strictly following [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), because it is convenient for areas in frequent communication to keep the same time.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Area that observes a uniform standard time

This article is about time zones in general. For a list of time zones by country, see [List of time zones by country](/wiki/List_of_time_zones_by_country "List of time zones by country"). For more time zone lists, see [Lists of time zones](/wiki/Lists_of_time_zones "Lists of time zones"). For other uses, see [Time zone (disambiguation)](/wiki/Time_zone_(disambiguation) "Time zone (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/World_Time_Zones_Map.svg/500px-World_Time_Zones_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_Time_Zones_Map.svg)

Time zones of the world

A **time zone** is an area which observes a uniform [standard time](/wiki/Standard_time "Standard time") for legal, commercial, and social purposes. Time zones tend to follow the boundaries between [countries](/wiki/Country "Country") and their [subdivisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") instead of strictly following [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), because it is convenient for areas in frequent communication to keep the same time.

Each time zone is defined by a standard offset from [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC). The offsets range from [UTC−12:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9212:00 "UTC−12:00") to [UTC+14:00](/wiki/UTC%2B14:00 "UTC+14:00"), and are usually a whole number of hours, but a few zones are offset by an additional 30 or 45 minutes, such as in [India](/wiki/Indian_Standard_Time "Indian Standard Time") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal_Time "Nepal Time"). Some areas in a time zone may use a different offset for part of the year, typically one hour ahead during [spring](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)") and [summer](/wiki/Summer "Summer"), a practice known as [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") (DST).

## List of UTC offsets

Main article: [List of UTC time offsets](/wiki/List_of_UTC_time_offsets "List of UTC time offsets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/World_time_zones.svg/960px-World_time_zones.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_time_zones.svg)

Time zones of the world

In the table below, the locations that use daylight saving time are listed in their UTC offset when DST is *not* in effect. When DST is in effect, approximately during spring and summer, their UTC offset is increased by one hour (except for [Lord Howe Island](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island "Lord Howe Island"), where it is increased by 30 minutes). For example, during the DST period, [California](/wiki/California "California") observes [UTC−07:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9207:00 "UTC−07:00") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") observes [UTC+01:00](/wiki/UTC%2B01:00 "UTC+01:00").

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/World_time_clock_%28multiple_faces%29_2.5m_high.made_in_Dresden_1690%2C_Technical_Instrument_Museum%2C_Dresden.jpg/250px-World_time_clock_%28multiple_faces%29_2.5m_high.made_in_Dresden_1690%2C_Technical_Instrument_Museum%2C_Dresden.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_time_clock_(multiple_faces)_2.5m_high.made_in_Dresden_1690,_Technical_Instrument_Museum,_Dresden.jpg)

World time clock, 2.5 m high. Made in Dresden 1690. Technical Instrument Museum, Dresden

The apparent position of the Sun in the sky, and thus [solar time](/wiki/Solar_time "Solar time"), varies by location due to the [spherical](/wiki/Spherical "Spherical") shape of the Earth. This variation corresponds to four [minutes](/wiki/Minute "Minute") of time for every [degree](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") of longitude; for example, when it is solar noon in London, it is about 10 minutes before solar noon in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), which is about 2.5 degrees to the west.[[6]](#cite_note-7) Local solar time has been associated with civil timekeeping since the development of [sundials](/wiki/Sundial "Sundial"); for example, c.1500 BC, an [ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") sundial was used to measure laborers' work hours.[[7]](#cite_note-8) In the 2nd century BC, [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") developed a method to measure longitudinal distances by comparing local solar times when a [lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") was simultaneously observed at two locations. The work of Hipparchus is lost, but is known from citations in Strabo's Geographica, c. 7 AD.

The [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), founded in 1675, established [Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time") (GMT), the mean solar time at that location, as an aid to mariners to determine longitude at sea, providing a standard reference time while each location in [England](/wiki/England "England") kept a different time.

### Railway time

Main article: [Railway time](/wiki/Railway_time "Railway time")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Time_zone_chicago.jpg/250px-Time_zone_chicago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Time_zone_chicago.jpg)

Plaque commemorating the Railway General Time Convention of 1883 in North America

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Coventry_Time_Zone_Clock.jpg/250px-Coventry_Time_Zone_Clock.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coventry_Time_Zone_Clock.jpg)

The control panel of the Time Zone Clock in front of [Coventry Transport Museum](/wiki/Coventry_Transport_Museum "Coventry Transport Museum")

In the 19th century, as transportation and telecommunications improved, it became increasingly inconvenient for each location to observe its own solar time. In November 1840, the British [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") started using GMT kept by portable [chronometers](/wiki/Marine_chronometer "Marine chronometer").[[8]](#cite_note-9)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] This practice was soon adopted by other British railway companies and became known as **railway time**.

Around August 23, 1852, time signals were first transmitted by [telegraph](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph") from the Royal Observatory. By 1855, 98% of Great Britain's public clocks were using GMT, but it was not made the island's legal time until August 2, 1880. Some British clocks from this period have two minute hands, one for the local time and one for GMT.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

On November 2, 1868, the British [Colony of New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") officially adopted a standard time to be observed throughout the colony.[[10]](#cite_note-11) It was based on longitude 172°30′ east of [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich "Greenwich"), or 11 hours 30 minutes ahead of GMT. This standard was known as [New Zealand Mean Time](/wiki/Time_in_New_Zealand "Time in New Zealand").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Time_zone_map_of_the_United_States_1913_%28colorized%29.png/500px-Time_zone_map_of_the_United_States_1913_%28colorized%29.png)](/wiki/File:Time_zone_map_of_the_United_States_1913_(colorized).png)

1913 time zone map of the United States, showing boundaries very different from today

Timekeeping on [North American](/wiki/North_America "North America") [railroads](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") in the 19th century was complex. Each railroad used its own standard time, usually based on the local time of its headquarters or most important terminus, and the railroad's train schedules were published using its own time. Some junctions served by several railroads had a clock for each railroad, each showing a different time.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Because of this, a number of accidents occurred when trains from different companies using the same tracks mistimed their passings.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Around 1863, [Charles F. Dowd](/wiki/Charles_F._Dowd "Charles F. Dowd") proposed a system of hourly standard time zones for North American railroads, although he published nothing on the matter at that time and did not consult railroad officials until 1869. In 1870, he proposed four ideal time zones having north–south borders, the first centered on [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."). By 1872, the first was centered on the meridian [75° west of Greenwich](/wiki/75th_meridian_west "75th meridian west"), with natural borders such as sections of the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"). Dowd's system was never accepted by North American railroads.
[Cleveland Abbe](/wiki/Cleveland_Abbe "Cleveland Abbe"), chief meteorologist at the [United States Weather Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Weather_Bureau "United States Weather Bureau"), divided the United States into four standard time zones for consistency among the weather stations. In 1879, he published a paper titled *Report on Standard Time*.[[14]](#cite_note-wws-15) In 1883, he convinced North American railroad companies to adopt his time-zone system. In 1884, Britain, which had already adopted its own standard time system for England, Scotland, and Wales, helped gather international consent for global time. In time, the American government, influenced in part by Abbe's 1879 paper, adopted the time-zone system.[[15]](#cite_note-16)
It was a version proposed by William F. Allen, the editor of the *Traveler's Official Railway Guide*.[[16]](#cite_note-17) The borders of its time zones ran through railroad stations, often in major cities. For example, the border between its Eastern and Central time zones ran through [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), and [Charleston](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina"). It was inaugurated on Sunday, November 18, 1883, also called "The Day of Two Noons",[[17]](#cite_note-18) when each railroad station clock was reset as standard-time noon was reached within each time zone.

The North American zones were named Intercolonial, Eastern, Central, Mountain, and Pacific. Within a year 85% of all cities with populations over 10,000 (about 200 cities) were using standard time.[[18]](#cite_note-19) A notable exception was Detroit (located about halfway between the meridians of Eastern and Central time), which kept local time until 1900, then tried Central Standard Time, [local mean time](/wiki/Local_mean_time "Local mean time"), and [Eastern Standard Time](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") (EST) before a May 1915 ordinance settled on EST and was ratified by popular vote in August 1916. The confusion of times came to an end when standard time zones were formally adopted by the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") in the [Standard Time Act](/wiki/Standard_Time_Act "Standard Time Act") of March 19, 1918.

### Worldwide time zones

"World time" redirects here. For the global time standard, see [Universal Time](/wiki/Universal_Time "Universal Time").

[Italian](/wiki/Italy "Italy") mathematician [Quirico Filopanti](/wiki/Quirico_Filopanti "Quirico Filopanti") introduced the idea of a worldwide system of time zones in his book *Miranda!*, published in 1858. He proposed 24 hourly time zones, which he called "longitudinal days", the first centred on the meridian of Rome. He also proposed a universal time to be used in astronomy and telegraphy. However, his book attracted no attention until long after his death.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)

[Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")-born [Canadian](/wiki/Canada "Canada") Sir [Sandford Fleming](/wiki/Sandford_Fleming "Sandford Fleming") proposed a worldwide system of time zones in 1876 - see [Sandford Fleming § Inventor of worldwide standard time](/wiki/Sandford_Fleming#Inventor_of_worldwide_standard_time "Sandford Fleming"). The proposal divided the world into twenty-four time zones labeled A-Y (skipping J), each one covering 15 degrees of longitude. All clocks within each zone would be set to the same time as the others, but differed by one hour from those in the neighboring zones.[[21]](#cite_note-22) He advocated his system at several international conferences, including the [International Meridian Conference](/wiki/International_Meridian_Conference "International Meridian Conference"), where it received some consideration. The system has not been directly adopted, but some maps divide the world into 24 time zones and assign letters to them, similarly to Fleming's system.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/World_Time_Zone_Chart_1942.jpg/500px-World_Time_Zone_Chart_1942.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_Time_Zone_Chart_1942.jpg)

World map of time zones in 1928

By about 1900, almost all inhabited places on Earth had adopted a standard time zone, but only some of them used an hourly offset from GMT. Many applied the time at a local astronomical observatory to an entire country, without any reference to GMT. It took many decades before all time zones were based on some standard offset from GMT or [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC). By 1929, the majority of countries had adopted hourly time zones, though some countries such as Iran, India, Myanmar, and parts of Australia had time zones with a 30-minute offset. Nepal was the last country to adopt a standard offset, shifting slightly to UTC+05:45 in 1986.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

All nations currently use standard time zones for secular purposes, but not all of them apply the concept as originally conceived. Several countries and subdivisions use half-hour or quarter-hour deviations from standard time. Some countries, such as China and India, use a single time zone even though the extent of their territory far exceeds the ideal 15° of longitude for one hour; other countries, such as Spain and Argentina, use standard hour-based offsets, but not necessarily those that would be determined by their geographical location. The consequences, in some areas, can affect the lives of local citizens, and in extreme cases contribute to larger political issues, such as in the western reaches of China.[[24]](#cite_note-25) In [Russia, which has 11 time zones](/wiki/Time_in_Russia "Time in Russia"), two time zones were removed in 2010[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) (one year before the daylight saving time would be observed all year round on March 27 of the following year) and reinstated in 2014 (when the country transferred from daylight saving time or summer time back to standard time or winter time permanently).[[27]](#cite_note-28)

## Notation

### ISO 8601

Main article: [ISO 8601](/wiki/ISO_8601 "ISO 8601")

ISO 8601 is a standard established by the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") defining methods of representing dates and times in textual form, including specifications for representing time zones.

If a time is in UTC, a "Z" is added directly after the time without a separating space. "Z" is the zone designator for the zero UTC offset. "09:30 UTC" is therefore represented as "09:30Z" or "0930Z". Likewise, "14:45:15 UTC" is written as "14:45:15Z" or "144515Z".[[28]](#cite_note-ZuluTime-29) UTC time is also known as "Zulu" time, since "Zulu" is a [phonetic alphabet](/wiki/ICAO_spelling_alphabet "ICAO spelling alphabet") code word for the letter "Z".[[28]](#cite_note-ZuluTime-29)

[Offsets from UTC](/wiki/UTC_offset "UTC offset") are written in the format ±hh:mm, ±hhmm, or ±hh (either hours ahead or behind UTC). For example, if the time being described is one hour ahead of UTC (such as the time in Germany during the winter), the zone designator would be "[+01:00](/wiki/UTC%2B01:00 "UTC+01:00")", "+0100", or simply "+01". This numeric representation of time zones is appended to local times in the same way that alphabetic time zone abbreviations (or "Z", as above) are appended. The offset from UTC changes with daylight saving time, e.g. a time offset in Chicago, which is in the [North American Central Time Zone](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone_(North_America) "Central Time Zone (North America)"), is "[−06:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9206:00 "UTC−06:00")" for the winter (Central Standard Time) and "[−05:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9205:00 "UTC−05:00")" for the summer (Central Daylight Time).[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### Abbreviations

Main article: [List of time zone abbreviations](/wiki/List_of_time_zone_abbreviations "List of time zone abbreviations")

Time zones are often represented by alphabetic abbreviations such as "EST", "WST", and "CST", though these are not part of the international time and date standard [ISO 8601](/wiki/ISO_8601#Time_zone_designators "ISO 8601"). Such designations can be ambiguous; for example, "CST" can mean (North American) [Central Standard Time](/wiki/Central_Standard_Time "Central Standard Time") (UTC−06:00), [Cuba Standard Time](/wiki/Cuba_Standard_Time "Cuba Standard Time") (UTC−05:00), or [China Standard Time](/wiki/China_Standard_Time "China Standard Time") (UTC+08:00), and is also a widely used variant of ACST ([Australian Central Standard Time](/wiki/Australian_Central_Standard_Time "Australian Central Standard Time"), UTC+09:30).[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Conversions

Conversion between time zones obeys the relationship

"time in zone A" − "UTC offset for zone A" = "time in zone B" − "UTC offset for zone B",

in which each side of the equation is equivalent to UTC.

The conversion equation can be rearranged to

"time in zone B" = "time in zone A" − "UTC offset for zone A" + "UTC offset for zone B".

For example, the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") opens at 09:30 ([EST](/wiki/Eastern_time_zone "Eastern time zone"), UTC offset= −05:00). In [California](/wiki/California "California") ([PST](/wiki/Pacific_time_zone "Pacific time zone"), UTC offset= −08:00) and [India](/wiki/India "India") ([IST](/wiki/Indian_standard_time "Indian standard time"), UTC offset= +05:30), the New York Stock Exchange opens at

time in California = 09:30 − (−05:00) + (−08:00) = 06:30;

time in India = 09:30 − (−05:00) + (+05:30) = 20:00.

These calculations become more complicated near the time switch to or from daylight saving time, as the UTC offset for the area becomes a function of UTC time.

The time differences may also result in different dates. For example, when it is 22:00 on Monday in Egypt (UTC+02:00), it is 01:00 on Tuesday in Pakistan (UTC+05:00).

## Nautical time zones

Main article: [Nautical time](/wiki/Nautical_time "Nautical time")

Since the 1920s, a [nautical standard time](/wiki/Nautical_time "Nautical time") system has been in operation for ships on the [high seas](/wiki/International_waters "International waters"). As an ideal form of the terrestrial time zone system, nautical time zones consist of [gores](/wiki/Gore_(segment) "Gore (segment)") of 15° offset from GMT by a whole number of hours. A nautical date line follows the 180th meridian, bisecting one 15° gore into two 7.5° gores that differ from GMT by ±12 hours.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)

However, in practice each ship may choose what time to observe at each location. Ships may decide to adjust their clocks at a convenient time, usually at night, not exactly when they cross a certain longitude.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Some ships simply remain on the time of the departing port during the whole trip.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

## Skewing of time zones

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Tzdiff-Europe-summer.png/250px-Tzdiff-Europe-summer.png)](/wiki/File:Tzdiff-Europe-summer.png)

Difference between sun time and clock time during daylight saving time:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | 1h ± 30 min behind |
|  | 0h ± 30m |
|  | 1h ± 30 m ahead |
|  | 2h ± 30 m ahead |
|  | 3h ± 30 m ahead |

Ideal time zones, such as nautical time zones, are based on the mean solar time of a particular meridian in the middle of that zone with boundaries located 7.5 degrees east and west of the meridian. In practice, however, many time zone boundaries are drawn much farther to the west, and some countries are located entirely outside their ideal time zones.

For example, even though the Prime Meridian (0°) passes through Spain and France they use the mean solar time of 15 degrees east ([Central European Time](/wiki/Central_European_Time "Central European Time")) rather than 0 degrees (Greenwich Mean Time). France previously used GMT, but was switched to CET (Central European Time) during the [German occupation](/wiki/German_occupation_of_France_during_World_War_II "German occupation of France during World War II") of the country during World War II and did not switch back after the war.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Similarly, prior to World War II, the Netherlands observed "Amsterdam Time", which was twenty minutes ahead of Greenwich Mean Time. They were obliged to follow German time during the war, and kept it thereafter. In the mid-1970s the Netherlands, as other European states, began observing daylight saving (summer) time.

One reason to draw time zone boundaries far to the west of their ideal meridians is to allow the more efficient use of afternoon sunlight.[[37]](#cite_note-38) Some of these locations also use daylight saving time, further increasing the difference to local solar time. As a result, in summer, solar noon in the Spanish city of [Vigo](/wiki/Vigo "Vigo") occurs at 14:41 clock time. This westernmost area of continental Spain never experiences sunset before 18:00 clock time, even in winter, despite lying 42 degrees north of the equator.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Near the summer [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice"), Vigo has sunset times after 22:00, similar to those of [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), which is in the same time zone and 17 degrees farther north. Stockholm has much earlier sunrises, though.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

In the United States, the reasons were more historical and business-related. In Midwestern states, like [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), those living in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") and [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") wanted to be on the same time zone as New York City to simplify communications and transactions.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

A more extreme example is [Nome, Alaska](/wiki/Nome,_Alaska "Nome, Alaska"), which is at 165°24′W longitude – just west of center of the idealized [Samoa Time Zone](/wiki/Samoa_Time_Zone "Samoa Time Zone") ([165°W](/wiki/165th_meridian_west "165th meridian west")). Nevertheless, Nome observes [Alaska Time](/wiki/Alaska_Time_Zone "Alaska Time Zone") ([135°W](/wiki/135th_meridian_west "135th meridian west")) with DST so it is slightly more than two hours ahead of the sun in winter and over three in summer.[[41]](#cite_note-42)
[Kotzebue, Alaska](/wiki/Kotzebue,_Alaska "Kotzebue, Alaska"), also near the same meridian but north of the Arctic Circle, has *two* sunsets on the same day in early August, one shortly after midnight at the start of the day, and the other shortly before midnight at the end of the day.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Consequently, in early May, in Kotzebue one day does not have sunset.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

[China extends as far west as 73°E](/wiki/List_of_extreme_points_of_China "List of extreme points of China"), but all parts of it use [UTC+08:00](/wiki/UTC%2B08:00 "UTC+08:00") ([120°E](/wiki/120th_meridian_east "120th meridian east")), so solar "noon" can occur as late as 15:00 in western portions of China such as [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang").[[44]](#cite_note-45) The [Afghanistan-China border](/wiki/Afghanistan-China_border "Afghanistan-China border") marks the greatest terrestrial time zone difference on Earth, with a 3.5 hour difference between Afghanistan's UTC+4:30 and China's [UTC+08:00](/wiki/Time_in_China "Time in China").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Solar_time_vs_standard_time.png/960px-Solar_time_vs_standard_time.png)](/wiki/File:Solar_time_vs_standard_time.png "A visualization of the mismatch between clock time and solar time in different locations. In blue areas, clock time lags behind solar time; in red areas, the reverse is true. The two are synchronized in the white areas.")

A visualization of the mismatch between clock time and solar time in different locations. In blue areas, clock time lags behind solar time; in red areas, the reverse is true. The two are synchronized in the white areas.

## Daylight saving time

Main article: [Daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/DaylightSaving-World-Subdivisions.png/500px-DaylightSaving-World-Subdivisions.png)](/wiki/File:DaylightSaving-World-Subdivisions.png)

DST observed

DST formerly observed

DST never observed

Many countries, and sometimes just certain regions of countries, adopt daylight saving time (DST), also known as summer time, during part of the year. This typically involves advancing clocks by an hour near the start of [spring](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)") and adjusting back in [autumn](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn") ("spring forward", "fall back"). Modern DST was first proposed in 1907 and was in widespread use in 1916 as a [wartime](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") measure aimed at [conserving coal](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation"). Despite [controversy](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time#Dispute_over_benefits_and_drawbacks "Daylight saving time"), many countries have used it off and on since then; [details vary by location](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time_by_country "Daylight saving time by country") and change occasionally. Countries around the equator usually do not observe daylight saving time, since the seasonal difference in sunlight there is minimal.

## Computer systems

Many computer [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_systems "Operating systems") include the necessary support for working with all many or all possible local times. Internally, operating systems typically use [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") as their basic [time-keeping standard](/wiki/System_time "System time") and convert local times, including [daylight savings time](/wiki/Daylight_savings_time "Daylight savings time"), to and from UTC.

Web servers presenting web pages primarily for an audience in a single time zone or a limited range of time zones typically show times as a local time, perhaps with UTC time in brackets. More internationally oriented websites may show times in UTC only or using an arbitrary time zone. For example, the international English-language version of CNN includes GMT and Hong Kong Time,[[45]](#cite_note-46) whereas the US version shows [Eastern Time](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone").[[46]](#cite_note-47)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]US Eastern Time and [Pacific Time](/wiki/Pacific_Time_Zone "Pacific Time Zone") are also used fairly commonly on many US-based English-language websites with global readership. The format is typically based in the [W3C](/wiki/World_Wide_Web_Consortium "World Wide Web Consortium") Note "datetime".

[Email](/wiki/Email "Email") systems and other messaging systems ([IRC chat](/wiki/Internet_Relay_Chat "Internet Relay Chat"), etc.)[[47]](#cite_note-48) time-stamp messages using UTC, or else include the sender's time zone as part of the message, allowing the receiving program to display the message's date and time of sending in the recipient's local time.

[Database](/wiki/Database "Database") records that include a time stamp typically use UTC, especially when the database is part of a system that spans multiple time zones. The use of local time for time-stamping records is not recommended for time zones that implement daylight saving time because once a year there is a one-hour period when local times are ambiguous.

[Calendar systems](/wiki/Calendaring_software "Calendaring software") nowadays usually tie their time stamps to UTC, and show them differently on computers that are in different time zones. That works when having telephone or internet meetings. It works less well when travelling, because the calendar events are assumed to take place in the time zone the computer or smartphone was on when creating the event. The event can be shown at the wrong time. For example, if a Los Angeles resident visits New York and before the journey makes a calendar entry for the return flight at 9 am (which the computer assumes is Los Angeles time), the calendar entry will be at noon if taking the computer's new New York time zone. Calendaring software must also deal with [daylight saving time](/wiki/Daylight_saving_time "Daylight saving time") (DST), usually done so calendar entries made before the shift are shown the same after it. If, for political reasons, the begin and end dates of daylight saving time are changed, calendar entries should stay the same in local time, even though they may shift in UTC time.

### Operating systems

#### Unix

Main article: [Unix time](/wiki/Unix_time "Unix time")

[Unix-like](/wiki/Unix-like "Unix-like") systems, including [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux") and [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"), keep system time in [Unix time](/wiki/Unix_time "Unix time") format, representing the number of seconds that have elapsed since 00:00:00 [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC) on Thursday, January 1, 1970, excluding [leap seconds](/wiki/Leap_second "Leap second").[[48]](#cite_note-single-unix-spec-4.16-49) Unix time is usually converted to local time when displayed to the user, and times specified by the user in local time are converted to Unix time. The conversion takes into account the time zone and daylight saving time rules; by default the time zone and daylight saving time rules are set up when the system is configured, though individual processes can specify time zones and daylight saving time rules using the *TZ* [environment variable](/wiki/Environment_variable "Environment variable").[[49]](#cite_note-tzset(3)-50) This allows users in multiple time zones, or in the same time zone but with different daylight saving time rules, to use the same computer, with their respective local times displayed correctly to each user. Information about time zones and daylight saving time rules most commonly comes from the [IANA time zone database](/wiki/IANA_time_zone_database "IANA time zone database"). Many systems, including anything using the [GNU C Library](/wiki/GNU_C_Library "GNU C Library"), a C library based on the [BSD](/wiki/BSD "BSD") C library, or the [System V Release 4](/wiki/System_V_Release_4 "System V Release 4") C library, can make use of the IANA time zone database.

#### Microsoft Windows

| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(July 2025)* |

[Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows")-based computer systems prior to [Windows 95](/wiki/Windows_95 "Windows 95") and [Windows NT](/wiki/Windows_NT "Windows NT") used local time, but Windows 95 and later, and Windows NT, base system time on UTC.[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) They allow a program to fetch the system time as UTC, represented as a year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and millisecond;[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) Windows 95 and later, and Windows NT 3.5 and later, also allow the system time to be fetched as a count of 100 ns units since 1601-01-01 00:00:00 UTC.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56) The [system registry](/wiki/Windows_Registry "Windows Registry") contains time zone information that includes the offset from UTC and rules that indicate the start and end dates for daylight saving in each zone. Interaction with the user normally uses local time, and application software is able to calculate the time in various zones. [Terminal Servers](/wiki/Remote_Desktop_Services "Remote Desktop Services") allow remote computers to redirect their time zone settings to the Terminal Server so that users see the correct time for their time zone in their desktop/application sessions. Terminal Services uses the server base time on the Terminal Server and the client time zone information to calculate the time in the session.

### Programming languages

#### Java

While most application software will use the underlying operating system for time zone and daylight saving time rule information, [the Java Platform](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)"), from version 1.3.1, has maintained its own database of time zone and daylight saving time rule information, based on the [IANA time zone database](/wiki/IANA_time_zone_database "IANA time zone database"). This database is updated whenever time zone or daylight saving time rules change. [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") provides an updater tool for this purpose.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

As an alternative to the information bundled with the Java Platform, programmers may choose to use the Joda-Time library.[[57]](#cite_note-58) This library includes its own data based on the IANA time zone database.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

As of Java 8 there is a new date and time API that can help with converting times.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

#### JavaScript

Traditionally, there was very little in the way of time zone support for [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript"). Essentially the programmer had to extract the UTC offset by instantiating a time object, getting a GMT time from it, and differencing the two. This does not provide a solution for more complex daylight saving variations, such as divergent DST directions between northern and southern hemispheres.

ECMA-402, the standard on Internationalization API for JavaScript, provides ways of formatting Time Zones.[[60]](#cite_note-61) However, due to size constraint, some implementations or distributions do not include it.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

#### Perl

The DateTime object in [Perl](/wiki/Perl "Perl") supports all entries in the [IANA time zone database](/wiki/IANA_time_zone_database "IANA time zone database") and includes the ability to get, set and convert between time zones.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

#### PHP

The DateTime objects and related functions have been compiled into the [PHP](/wiki/PHP "PHP") core since 5.2. This includes the ability to get and set the default script time zone, and DateTime is aware of its own time zone internally. PHP.net provides extensive documentation on this.[[63]](#cite_note-64) As noted there, the most current time zone database can be implemented via the [PECL](/wiki/PEAR#PECL "PEAR") timezonedb.

#### Python

The standard module datetime included with [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)") stores and operates on the time zone information class tzinfo. The third party pytz module provides access to the full IANA time zone database.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Negated time zone offset in seconds is stored time.timezone and time.altzone attributes. From Python 3.9, the zoneinfo module introduces timezone management without need for third party module.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

#### Smalltalk

Each [Smalltalk](/wiki/Smalltalk "Smalltalk") dialect comes with its own built-in classes for dates, times and timestamps, only a few of which implement the DateAndTime and Duration classes as specified by the ANSI Smalltalk Standard. [VisualWorks](/wiki/VisualWorks "VisualWorks") provides a TimeZone class that supports up to two annually recurring offset transitions, which are assumed to apply to all years (same behavior as Windows time zones). [Squeak](/wiki/Squeak "Squeak") provides a Timezone class that does not support any offset transitions. [Dolphin Smalltalk](/wiki/Dolphin_Smalltalk "Dolphin Smalltalk") does not support time zones at all.

For full support of the tz database (zoneinfo) in a Smalltalk application (including support for any number of annually recurring offset transitions, and support for different intra-year offset transition rules in different years) the third-party, open-source, ANSI-Smalltalk-compliant Chronos Date/Time Library is available for use with any of the following Smalltalk dialects: VisualWorks, Squeak, Gemstone, or Dolphin.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

## Time in outer space

Orbiting spacecraft may experience many sunrises and sunsets, or none, in a 24-hour period. Therefore, it is not possible to calibrate the time with respect to the Sun and still respect a 24-hour sleep/wake cycle. A common practice for [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration") is to use the Earth-based time of the launch site or mission control, synchronizing the sleeping cycles of the crew and controllers. The [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") normally uses Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)

[Timekeeping on Mars](/wiki/Timekeeping_on_Mars "Timekeeping on Mars") can be more complex, since the planet has a solar day of approximately 24 hours and 40 minutes, known as a [sol](/wiki/Sol_(day_on_Mars) "Sol (day on Mars)"). Earth controllers for some Mars missions have synchronized their sleep/wake cycles with the Martian day, when specifically solar-powered rover activity occurs.[[69]](#cite_note-70)
